chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Any general motion of a rigid body = translation of CM + rotation about CM
Velocity of any point P: v_P = v_cm + ω × r_P (vector sum)
The CM moves as if all external forces act on it at the CM
Rotation about CM follows τ_cm = I_cm × α (torques about CM)
Translation and rotation can be analysed independently using Newton's laws
For pure rolling: v_cm = ωR, linking the two motions
truth_statements: The most general motion of a rigid body is a combination of translation and rotation
The velocity of any point on the body is the vector sum of the translational velocity of the CM and the rotational velocity about the CM
The kinetic energy of a rigid body in general motion is the sum of translational KE of the CM and rotational KE about the CM
Newton's second law for the CM: F_net = Ma_cm treats the body as a particle at the CM
The rotational equation τ_cm = I_cm α governs the rotation about the CM independently

aha_moment.what_to_show: A thrown baton in the air: CM follows a parabolic path (like a point particle) while the baton rotates uniformly about the CM. The two motions are superimposed — every point on the baton follows a complex cycloid-like path, but the CM is a clean parabola.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: CM path (clean parabola) highlighted in one colour, a tip's complex path in another — decomposition made visual
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think all points of a rotating-translating body follow complicated paths — seeing the CM as a simple parabola makes decomposition intuitive

simulation_states.label: Pure translation
simulation_states.description: A rod translating without rotation. Every point has velocity v_cm. CM path and endpoint paths are identical parallel lines.
simulation_states.emphasis: In pure translation, all points have the same velocity

simulation_states.label: Pure rotation about CM
simulation_states.description: Same rod rotating about its CM (no translation). CM is stationary. Endpoints trace circles. v_P = ω × r.
simulation_states.emphasis: In pure rotation about CM, the CM doesn't move

simulation_states.label: Combined motion
simulation_states.description: Rod thrown in the air: CM follows parabola, rod rotates about CM. Each endpoint traces a cycloid. v_P = v_cm + ω × r shown as vector sum at one point.
simulation_states.emphasis: General motion = translation of CM + rotation about CM — always decomposable

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student throws a baton with adjustable v_cm and ω. Trace paths of CM and endpoints. KE bar splits into translational and rotational parts.
simulation_states.emphasis: Adjust spin and throw speed — CM always follows a parabola regardless of spin

common_misconceptions.belief: The centre of mass doesn't follow a simple path when the body rotates
common_misconceptions.correction: The CM always follows the path dictated by F_net = Ma_cm, regardless of rotation. A spinning baton in projectile motion has its CM follow a perfect parabola — the spin doesn't affect the CM trajectory.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Highlight the CM path as a clean parabola while endpoints trace complex curves

common_misconceptions.belief: You can't separate translation and rotation — they affect each other
common_misconceptions.correction: For a rigid body, translation of CM (F = Ma) and rotation about CM (τ = Iα) are independent equations. They can be solved separately and then superimposed.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show translation equation and rotation equation side by side — solved independently

legend.meaning: Velocity of point P = v_cm + ω×r
